ontology.foodon_label: meat and grain porridge food product
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Haleem specifically. The closest defensible FoodOn class is a meat-and-grain porridge food product. URI cited is the closest parent anchor; no fabricated code used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based composite dish — slow-cooked meat and cereal porridge
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Haleem code. A0CKP (composite meat dish) is the closest defensible category anchor in the FoodEx2 hierarchy for a slow-cooked meat-and-grain preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Haleem or a sufficiently close food concept. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

preparation_method: seasoning with ghee and spice mix
main_ingredients: haleem spice mix (cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, cumin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Haleem is approximately 250 g (one bowl), which is the typical restaurant and home portion size across Hyderabad and South Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR-NIN, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Haleem as a standalone entry. Values are adapted from NIN data for slow-cooked meat-and-lentil composite dishes combined with broken wheat and barley components. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.8 g per 100 g based on ghee and mutton fat contribution ratios documented in NIN tables. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and water values are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this specific preparation are not individually reported in NIN or USDA FoodData Central for Haleem as a composite dish.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — analogous estimate for slow-cooked meat-and-lentil-wheat porridge
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Haleem exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with slow-cooked lentil-and-wheat porridge entries (GI range 35–50) documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables. The slow-cooking process, high dietary fibre from four lentil types (4.2 g/100 g), high protein (11.8 g/100 g), and fat from ghee (10.5 g/100 g) all attenuate glucose absorption, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (45 × 18.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 20.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Haleem to small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced ghee and salt preparation, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 775 mg sodium — over 30% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45) offset by high caloric density (215 kcal/100 g) and moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Haleem's low estimated GI and high protein and fibre content support relatively stable postprandial glucose; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150–200 g, prefer lean meat versions with reduced ghee, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to manage overall glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and red meat, and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from ghee and mutton (estimated ~3.8 g/100 g) and sodium of 310 mg/100 g are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose chicken or lean mutton versions, request minimal ghee, and restrict serving size to ≤150 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon) balanced against pro-inflammatory saturated fat from ghee
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Slow-cooked meat and lentils provide high-quality amino acids beneficial for joint tissue repair, and spices such as ginger and cinnamon have documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, excessive ghee should be avoided as saturated fat may exacerbate systemic inflammation in some individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Haleem is a highly nutritious, energy-dense slow-cooked porridge that is an iconic dish of Hyderabad, India, with roots in Persian Harees. It is prepared by simmering meat — traditionally mutton or beef, though chicken versions are common — together with broken wheat (daliya), barley, and four types of lentils (chana dal, masoor dal, moong dal, and urad dal) for 8 to 12 hours over low heat. Throughout cooking, the mixture is continuously stirred and pounded with heavy wooden mashers called ghoatna until the meat fibres completely dissolve and meld with the grains, producing a distinctive elastic, paste-like, homogeneous texture unlike any other South Asian dish. The finished Haleem is seasoned with pure ghee and a specialized spice blend including cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, black pepper, and cumin, then garnished with fried onions, fresh coriander, lemon juice, and ginger julienne. Per 100 g, Haleem provides approximately 215 kcal, 11.8 g protein, 10.5 g fat, 18.4 g carbohydrate, and 4.2 g dietary fibre, making it one of the most protein- and fibre-rich traditional South Asian dishes. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 (low) reflects the combined effect of slow cooking, high fibre from lentils, and fat and protein from meat and ghee slowing glucose absorption. Clinically, Haleem is suitable in moderate portions for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, but its sodium content (310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee require portion control for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Haleem is especially associated with Ramadan Iftar meals, winter consumption, and post-illness recovery across Hyderabad, Pakistan, and the broader South Asian diaspora. It is listed as a Geographical Indication (GI) product by the Government of India for Hyderabad.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of Haleem?
What is Hyderabadi Haleem made of?
How is Haleem different from Harees?
Can I eat Haleem if I have high blood pressure?
What lentils are used in Haleem?
How long does it take to cook Haleem?
Is Haleem good for weight loss?
What is ghoatna in Haleem preparation?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for slow-cooked meat-and-lentil-wheat composite dishes; Haleem is not listed as a standalone entry in NIN or USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar slow-cooked lentil-wheat porridges. Saturated fat is estimated from component fat ratios. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Haleem-specific nutrient profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for Haleem as a slow-cooked composite dish are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; these fields are therefore set to -1 to indicate data not available rather than confirmed zero. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100 g based on ghee and mutton fat contribution ratios from NIN component tables. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for slow-cooked lentil-and-wheat porridges (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct Haleem GI measurement is available in any approved source. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 18.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 20.7 g. The input record contained four duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. The input source 'South Asian Health Foundation / sahf.org.uk' for GI is not on the approved source list; replaced with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate per source policy. Haleem is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; mandatory multilingual script requirements for those cuisines do not apply. Hindi script (हलीम) is provided as the primary South Asian language form. Urdu (حلیم) and Telugu (హలీమ్) are included as regional alias forms. No fabricated ontology codes used; FoodOn and FoodEx2 entries are closest_parent anchors with gap notes. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' as the codes represent the nearest parent category in each hierarchy rather than an exact match for Haleem specifically.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g3_numeric_hygiene — expanded notes field to explicitly list every field set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g), state the reason data is unavailable (not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for Haleem as a composite dish as of 2024), and cite the sources consulted that confirmed unavailability. This satisfies the G3 negative sentinel values documentation requirement.

Iteration 3: Fixed g5_ontology_honesty — changed foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. The validator requires match_type values from the set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'approximate' is not a valid value. Both ontology entries are correctly classified as closest_parent anchors since no exact Haleem-specific code exists in either FoodOn or FoodEx2.
